text: She was brought to the operating room and placed a pine on the table.
A midline laparotomy incision was performed.
Dissection was carried down past the subcutaneous tissue and the linea alba was incised to enter the peritoneal cavity.
Upon entering the peritoneal cavity, the small bowel and right colon were found to be very ischemic, but not frankly necrotic or perforated.
The falciform ligament is taken down and ligated.
The liver is noted to be quite cirrhotic.
To identify the SMA at the root of the mesenteric, the colon is retracted superiorly and the small intestine is retracted inferiorly.
The calcified superior mesenteric artery can be palpated at the root of the mesenteric.
There is no pulse in the artery, but feeling the calcification, dissection is carried down to the SMA.
The artery is dissected circumferentially and proximal control is obtained with the vessel loop.
Further dissection is carried out so that all of the SMA branches are dissected free and controlled with vessel loops.
Ideally, we would have taken our inflow for our bypass from the supraceliac aorta.
To expose the left external iliac artery, we took down the line of TOLTS along the sigmoid colon.
This allowed us to mobilize the sigmoid colon medially and expose the retroperitoneal structures.
Through this approach, we are able to identify the external iliac artery and dissect the artery circumferentially, obtaining control with the vessel loop.
As pointed out here, the psoas muscle, external iliac artery, and external iliac vein can be visualized in the field.
The artery is noted to be soft in this area, amenable for clamping.
Once proximal and distal control of the external iliac artery has been obtained, we can then create a tunnel through the sigmoid mesocolon.
This tunnel is performed with blunt finger dissection as well as with a hemostat, being careful not to injure any of the vessels in the mesocolon.
The tunnel is then secured with an umbilical tape.
Because we anticipate that the patient will require bowel resection in the future, we elected to perform our bypass using greater saphenous vein.
The greater saphenous vein is harvested from the left thigh as can be seen here.
The vein is ligated distally and proximally and it is marked and pressurized on the back table.
Any leaking branches are ligated with silk ties.
Once satisfied with our vein, the external iliac artery is clamped proximally and distally with padded fulgury clamps and an arteriotomy is made.
An end-to-side anastomosis is created between the vein and the artery using 6-0 running proline suture.
The clamps are released and the vein is pressurized to test the anastomosis.
Care is taken here to prevent twisting as the vein is tunneled through the mesocolon.
The vein will be brought through the mesocolon and to the left of the small bowel and over the small bowel mesentery to reach the SMA.
As we'll be seeing later, this classic C-loop configuration is prone to kinking when using vein.
After clamping the SMA proximally and distally, an arteriotomy is made with an 11 blade and extended with pot scissors.
The bypass graft is pressurized and it is brought up over the small bowel and to the SMA.
Once happy with the way that the vein lays, it is marked and it is cut to appropriate length.
An end-to-side anastomosis is then performed between the vein and the SMA using 6-0 proline suture in a running fashion.
The clamps are released and there is noted to be a strong pulse in the bypass as well as a pulse in the SMA distally.
All appears well initially.
However, once the retractors are released, more specifically the transverse colon retractor,
we note that there is a sharp kink at the anastomosis.
This happened because the area on the SMA that was soft where we were able to place our anastomosis was also very proximal.
As the transverse mesocolon is released, it comes down inferiorly and kinks the vein.
To solve this problem, we decided to abandon the C-loop configuration
and take our bypass in a straight line from the external iliac artery to the SMA.
In order to do this, we create a tunnel in the small bowel mesentery through which our bypass will travel.
Care is taken that the small vessels traveling in the small bowel mesentery are not injured during the creation of our tunnel.
The tunnel is secured with umbilical tape.
We carefully take down our SMA anastomosis using an 11-blade scalpel.
Once taken down, the vein can be tunneled through the small bowel mesentery.
It is then brought up to the SMA in a straight line configuration without the C-loop.
An end-to-side anastomosis is once again created between the greater saphenous vein graft and the SMA using 6-0 proline suture in a running fashion.
The clamps are released and the vein is noted to lay much better in this configuration.
The bypass and distal SMA are examined with a Doppler probe.
and are noted to have strong low resistance Doppler waveforms.
The retractors are released and there is no kinking in the graft.
The abdomen is temporarily closed with an Apthera Wundvac with plans to return to the operating room later for a second look to evaluate the viability of the small bowel.
